1.description: Dutch 30-20 year yield spread: UFR This graph shows the Dutch 30-20 year government bond yield spread in percentage points around the announcement (and implementation) date of the UFR. The vertical lines represent 5 days before and after the announcement (and implementation) of the UFR on July 2, 2012.

2.description: Change in the regulatory discount curve Panel A shows the economic discount curve (solid red line) and the regulatory discount curve (dashed green line) at implementation of the UFR on July 2, 2012. The graph also shows the economic (solid blue line) and regulatory (dotted black line) discount curve after a parallel shock in market interest rates of △yt=−1%⁠. Panel B shows the economic discount curve (solid red line) and the regulatory discount curve (solid green line) at implementation of the UFR on July 2, 2012. The graph also shows the economic (dashed blue line) and regulatory (dotted black line) discount curve after a change in the 20-year market interest rate of △yt(20)=−0.5%⁠.

3.description: Long-term bond holdings over time This graph shows the cross-sectional average fraction of the bond portfolio that is invested in 30-year bonds, that is, bonds with a maturity of 30 years or longer (panel A), and for the fraction that is invested in bonds with maturities close to 20 years, that is, between 15 and 25 years (panel B). The sample of P&Is is split in those with long liability durations (above the median) and for those with short liability durations (below the median). The bond portfolio contains the investments in all type of bonds and across all issuer countries. The fractions are in percentage points and the quarterly sample period is 2009q1-2019q4.

4.description: Weights of investor types within maturity buckets This figure displays the average weights of the investor types (banks, insurance companies, foreign investors, mutual funds, pension funds, and other investors) that held Dutch debt the year prior to the regulatory reform 2011q1-2012q1 (panel A) and the year after the regulatory reform 2012q3-2013q3 (panel B). The banks, mutual funds, and other investor types are at the euro area level. Dutch pension funds, Dutch insurance companies, and the P&I sector in the euro area excluding the Netherlands are separately reported. The foreign sector is determined as the fraction of total amount outstanding that is not held by euro area investors. The weights are in percentage points.

5.description: Exposures to interest rates: P&Is versus banks This figure shows the net exposure of P&Is and banks’ swap positions. For the Dutch P&I sector, I compute the aggregate net exposure of their swap positions per maturity bucket. For the Dutch banks, I compute the aggregate net exposures they have with the Dutch P&I sector. The notional amounts to compute the net exposures are based on Euribor plain vanilla swaps. The data come from EMIR and averaged over the period 2018q1-2019q4.

6.description: Bond holdings pre/post-implementation of the European-wide UFR Panels A and B show the average fraction of the bond portfolio invested in 30-year bonds (⁠T≥30⁠) and the fraction invested in bonds with maturities close to 20 years, that is, T∈(15,25]⁠. The cross-sectional averages are computed both pre- and post-announcement of the European-wide UFR. Panels C and D show the cross-sectional average fraction of the country’s bonds outstanding held by the insurance sector in that country, again for the two different maturity buckets. Panels A and C show the results for those countries that applied mark-to-market valuation of the liabilities prior to the UFR (ie, Ireland, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands) and panels B and D for those that used historical costs valuation (Austria, Belgium, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Italy, Portugal, Slovakia, and Spain). Finland, Portugal, and Slovakia are excluded from panel D, because for substantial periods of time they are lacking outstanding bonds within either the T∈(15,25] or T≥30 maturity bucket. The average allocations pre-UFR (post-UFR) for each country are computed as the time-series average allocation over the period 2009q1-2015q2 (2015q3-2019q4).
